transcript.text: Played, I was just, we had an in-person prospect meeting.
And so, you know, in-person is like never as easy to like wrap it up, you know?
Oh, yeah.
Gotta have to walk out and everything.
Yeah, yeah.
Where are you based?
Chicago.
Nice, nice.
I think we have one person in Chicago, Jacob, but he's moving to, he's moving.
Where is he moving to?
He's moving somewhere.
But anyways, are you originally from Chicago?
Well, originally from Iowa, but I've been in Chicago for 15 years.
Nice, nice.
That's awesome.
How'd you first hear about us?
Truthfully, I track kind of up-and-coming startups on the official Lean AI Native Companies leaderboard.
Okay, cool.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So, saw you guys pop up on there and then started seeing if you had any job openings.
Applied with one with Tucker.
And then you guys filled it like immediately afterwards.
He's like, do you mind if we just stay in touch?
like, yeah, absolutely.
Oh, that's so cool, man.
That's awesome.
Yeah, Henry reached out to me or like we were interacting and then I invited him over because one of our customers, Surge AI, we were doing kind of some stuff with them.
And then I was like, hey, you should talk to them.
They're literally the leanest of companies, like a billion in ARR and stuff.
then like we've been in touch and we're trying to plan out an AI growth summit together, too.
So that's cool.
Yeah.
Nice.
Yeah, that's awesome.
How's it been the interview experience so far?
I know you've talked to a bunch of people, heard amazing things about you.
So how's it been on your side experience so far?
Yeah, it's been great.

Candidly, I've been in other interview processes and this one has by far been the most enjoyable from a candidate experience, both like getting to meet different team members, getting like thoughtful questions, having like a real project to work on.
And some of the others are just kind of lackluster.
And so, yeah, it's been great.
Awesome.
Well, never a dull moment when it comes to startups, you know?
Oh, yeah, definitely.
Same thing with CDOs, just like never a dull moment.
But, well, maybe before jumping in, always like to turn the tables a little bit.
If there's parts about the company that can help give you some color or questions that have popped up that you feel like we have an answer versus like taking up all the time since we've taken a lot.
I always like to give back a little bit first, you know?
Yeah, I mean, I think some of my bigger questions are just like over the next, you know, year, two years, kind of like, what's the biggest opportunity to deepen the value with existing clients?
I don't know if it's like extending new offerings.
I know there's definitely a product in place to kind of make it more self-service.
But I just kind of wanted to hear from your end, like, what's the opportunity with existing clients versus always chasing new?
Yeah.
So I think the main thing right now is you have every single website on the planet is being disrupted.
So what that means is it's not like search is going to zero or, you know, websites are going away by any means, but there is this whole other vector.
Same way when, you know, mobile or social came up, right?
Like it became a whole thing that then just took everyone's attention.
And now it's not like social is the only place people spend time.
And for a while, everyone's like, oh, social, like, you know, this is it.
Like it's only social, right?
Like, and nobody was even thinking about anything else for a minute, right?

And I think this is a bigger shift than that, but specifically for websites, it makes it to where the content that they have on their websites and what they say and how they say it and what they do becomes even more important because they're just not going to exist and not going to be able to tell their message whatsoever if, you know, they can't control it.
Like, it's not like they.
We can go into a marketplace and bid to show up on answers in LLMs today.
So there's no cheating around it.
There's no like, let me just, you know, do this thing and just pay to play.
There's none of that right now, right?
And so what that means is like, there's the only thing you can do is actually get really good at content on your website.
And so, and then a lot of companies, they just kind of thought of content as an afterthought, right?
And so now you have this entire market, every single CEO, every single CMO thinking, what do I do with this?
What the hell am I supposed to do?
And a lot of these players like that popped up in this kind of LLM visibility space kind of got a lot of attention.
But what they're doing is tracking and not actually helping you do the work in order to impact that, right?
For instance, like one of our customers that we've had it for a long time.
I'm Ramp.
We've literally been working with them for over a year.
And one of the biggest players that raised a ton of money in the LLM visibility said they increase Ramp's visibility by 7x.
And all the screenshots they have in their customer case study are all content that literally we worked on.
We published, we did.
What company was that?
Profound.
Yeah, I saw them last week.
I was like, there's got to be somebody out there doing this.
And so it's crazy.
So they're like the hottest company.
All the VCs are hitting me up.
Hey, have you heard of Profound?
Everybody's trying to invest in them, right?
And their biggest story was this, right?
And so I think that's part of the opportunity for us.
Like we're building that part that the LLM tracking, it's like a very thin layer.
Honestly, it's not a hard layer to build at all.

And so we see there's kind of this opportunity to not miss out on that side since so many people are thinking about the visibility so much and the tracking and because we have everything else and it plays into the space so well.
Because, and I can, if we have time, I can show you some stuff too.
But yeah, so that's like a big one.
And then the other one is the fact that, I don't know if you saw like Clay just raised 100 million at 3 billion value.
And these things are not getting any easier.
Like I've used Clay other aspects.
And so like what we're seeing is the workflow building and turning these workflows into agentic as well is not an easy thing to do.
And a lot of what we built internally for our zone platform is almost kind of this like cursor meets lovable for building these workflows and running these workflows.
So there's this much bigger platform play here as well.
And our customers keep asking to get access to a lot of it.
We're just being very thoughtful to make sure that it's at a point where our internal folks are using every day and getting a lot of value and it's good.
And then we can expose it externally.
It's just a different way to approach it versus just like releasing it to the world and then, you know, you got all this noise and, you know, you're not.
Validating that it works.
Instead, we extract way more revenue because it's serviced, and then we use the platform internally.
And as we validate the platform, then we can, you know, distribute it more broadly.
Yeah.
And then more specifically, I think for, like, scaling client success, is it, right now, I know there's, like, great content distribute and convert.
How do you see that kind of scaling within existing clients?
Or is it, like, really about finding new clients in the mid-market and really scaling with them to kind of get optimized and then converting them to the platform?
Yeah.
Like, I think right now, the part of the challenge is, like, we're extracting a lot of value already.
Like, our average deal says, like, we have customers paying us anywhere from $9,000 to $60,000 a month.
And, like,
And we have one that is in contracts to potentially pay us $2.30 a month.
And so, like, it's pretty large contract values.
And the content piece is really important because of the foundation for everything else.
And what we're starting to see more and more is that the more mature programs, they also need to think about how do you build distribution.
So, for example, for Augment Code, like we're doing a newsletter for them that then helps them control that more.
Like, can't control when someone goes into an LLM and queries something.
You can't control when somebody goes in search and searches for something.
You can control sending an email to your subscribers.
You can control getting in front of, like, people that follow you on social, right?
Like, so then, like, if you have that distribution, that audience, then you can control that more.
And then finally, it's, like, how do you create offers that are kind of irresistible so that people, one, become subscribers, but also kind of get closer and closer to potentially.
You know, spending money and monetizing.
And so like, we're, we're kind of like sequentially getting there.
It's just that you, if you start with conversion, and you don't, don't know how to get to growing your audience organically, then you won't succeed long term.
And, and I think for us, like the way we're thinking about it is like, we can deliver the service, we can deliver value.
And there's a lot of things that the service will close that gap.
And, but then the platform will help us like, turn around and make it way more sticky, right?

Like, so for instance, like a lot of the things we're doing right now is like a custom knowledge base to ingest a lot of information, like an, like assistance, or, you know, a database of opportunities to identify opportunities within your website that needs to be improved, or, you know, the LLM tracking, but then ingesting data from Google Search Console, as well as an analytics and, you know, correlating that there's a lot of those things that once we open up to customers.
They, it will be a lot more, more sticky.
We just don't want to get ahead of it too, too fast, you know, and then it becomes, because right now, like the biggest thing is when you kick off a new engagement, people just don't know.
They don't have any context.
They don't have any good artifacts.
You ask them, hey, can you send me a description of your products?
They don't have it.
Hey, can you, who's your audience?
They don't know.
Like who, who's your persona?
They don't know.
They, how do you explain what your company does?
They're not very good at.
So, so if you gave someone a platform, no matter how good that platform is, they just wouldn't be successful because of that, you know?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Totally get that.
Cause we're the company I'm currently at.
We help people with AI automations and kind of workflows.
And we're just like, do you have a standard operating procedure for this role?
And they're like, no.
Yeah.
Um, so yeah, get that.
There's, there's a big lift trying to get people from zero to one and especially for a new platform, getting them to kind of understand all the nuance, how to use the best practices, et cetera.
So.
Totally get that.
But changing gears a little bit, just really curious as well, like what would you say is kind of your superpower?
You know, you've been in a lot of different cool companies and done the role in different ways as well.
But curious how you see your superpower.
Yeah, I'd say like curiosity is probably my biggest one.
Like I'm just ultimately the most curious person at the end of the day of like, why do things work the way they do?
Why have you set things up this way?

But I think that curiosity lends itself to just make everyone around me better because I'm asking questions that may be dumb, but it's like we need to get the obvious out there in front of everyone so that there's no assumptions that we miss.
But yeah, I think curiosity leads the way for me most of the time.
And then that curiosity, allowing other people around me to just do better.
Nice.
And what drives you absolutely insane?
At work, when something happens or, you know, when the client engagement or anything like that, like what, what annoys you or what, you know, bothers you if it's not, you know.
Yeah, I'd say like waiting is probably the thing that I hate the most.
It's just like, if we gave a deadline and the deadline's approaching, I haven't heard from anyone.
There's no status update.
I'm just like, what the  is going on?
And just really trying to be like, okay, there's the deadline approaching and we still don't really know.
It's a gray area.
So then I start pinging people and bothering them in the middle of their workflow.
So I think, yeah, waiting or even waiting on clients to deliver like some of the SOP documentation or let's give us some examples of these emails that we're trying to automate for you.
I've been waiting on one client for like three weeks just to send me like five emails as an example.
So that's usually kind of my pet peeve.
But I try to work around it by being more proactive and just weekly updates like, hey, still waiting on this, you know, as soon as you can send it over.
But yeah, waiting is definitely hard for me.
That makes sense.
Okay, so what's what a customer engagement rate, like either your current job or your last one that you feel like was probably one of the engagements you enjoyed the most working with a customer?
Yeah.
probably one for my previous role at Swallum when I was in consulting, working with the CMO at, I'm blanking on the name, Royal Caribbean.
It was 2022, and it was like during the hype cycle of Metaverse.

And so I got to come in as like a sole person program manager to come in and like wrangle four different external teams, plus their internal teams at Royal Caribbean, which never really talked to each other.
I kind of come in as like the subject matter expert on Metaverse, because I've just been doing a ton of reading and a bit of a nerd sometimes.
So came in.
I helped them just get a wrangle on, like, what is this thing that you're trying to build, which was essentially a digital twin of the new ship that they were launching.
Unfortunately, after two weeks of getting on the project, I kind of raised some red flags that, like, hey, guys, we don't have a budget for GPUs, which we will need to power this experience.
We currently have a $7 million budget.
It'll at least double that if we don't get in front of this.

And so I came in and knocked the whole thing off its knees and had to kind of shut it down and pivot it to, hey, since we can't literally afford to do the experience that we're going for that's already halfway built, let's go find, you know, our agency was one of the best in the world, Martin Agency.
And, like, let's go find somebody at Fortnite, go find somebody at Roblox and, like, say, here's what we have built so far.
We want to translate this into your world so that we can still kind of deliver the same experience, but maybe not at the same fidelity and definitely without the cloud.
But working with them was awesome.
mean, getting in Miami and getting out of Chicago in the winter was also pretty fun, to be honest.

But yeah, I think coming in there is just like a one-person crew and gaining the trust of the CMO to be able to like pivot their entire program for this new ship launch was definitely one of the most enjoyable things I've done in my career.
That's perfect.
Okay, so who would you say was your main point of contact on the customer side there?
Just the first name is fine.
Yeah, so Natalie Fernandez was the director of social, kind of who I worked with day to day.
And she was the one that was kind of the spearhead for the metaverse idea in the first place.
So she rolled up to the VP of marketing who worked up to take care of the CMO.
Nice.
Okay, so how would you say Natalie?
Yeah.
How would Natalie describe you?
Great question.
I think she followed up with me when I left because I did another social commerce project for her.
She said, you're brilliant.
We love working with you.
Tell us where you land and we'd love to work together again.
That was a couple of years ago.
So the AI space, they weren't so ready for when I left.
But yeah, at least she told me in writing, you know, we think you're brilliant.
We love working with you.
And what, like, what about the mindset as you come in?
you think that helps with, you know, getting a customer to be like that or feel like that about you?
You know, like, what's your mindset when you come into an engagement, you know, that to earn that level of trust and respect and love?
Yeah, it's, I mean, definitely, like I said, the curiosity, asking a lot of questions, even if they may make me look dumb at the beginning.
But trying to gain trust through just pure honesty and.
And advocacy for the client.
You know, a lot of times my management at the consulting firm, we're just like, what the hell are you doing over there?

We can't keep track of like all the different conversations you're having, but I'm sitting there talking to IT, telling them like, hey, we need to get a single sign on using your existing login credentials for RoyalPrivy.com to use for this experience.
And they're just like, no, there's no way that's going to happen.
Or like breaking down to the CMO, like here's the budget for going to get GPUs and, you know, I'm on your side for this.
I'm just telling you it's going to cost a lot of money.
But I think just being true to myself and being kind of a client servant is really the way that I earned trust.

And then it's more of like, okay, let me bring my actual skill set of being a consultant, being a delivery expert, being a technology subject matter expert, and then, you know, show off what I can do after I've gotten the kind of trust and candidacy.
Okay, so a couple more.
And then like, I'm happy to answer more, more questions too.
is super helpful.
What, like as part of your, your job, what's the most energizing part of it on a day-to-day and what's the most draining part in the day-to-day for you?
Yeah, I'd say right now we do a lot of workshops with clients, which I love to do.
Just kind of getting at the tip of the sphere of you guys don't understand AI and how it can work for your business.
Let's just start to show you some examples.
We do a lot of virtual, which I prefer in person, but neither here nor there.
But I think getting people from like the, I can never do that to, holy , you know, this looks like something I can do to at the end of the workshop.
Like, I know I can go do this is probably one of the more energizing parts of my day.
And we do at least like three of those a week right now.
The most draining part is probably the follow-on of like, how do we get all these different sticky notes?
And do a...

A reasonable summary of what happened today and doing like scribing of the sticky notes is probably one of the more draining and getting it to our solution delivery leads who like love everything in a perfect like input format for them to go do their output.
So I'd say it's definitely necessary, but one of the more draining parts is doing the scribing post-workup.
Nice, nice.
That makes sense.
Yeah, like, is there anything else you feel like on the day-to-day that if like in this next role if you didn't have to do it, it would be, you'd be very happy about never doing it again?
Oh, yeah.
I mean, like, immediate in-mail or inbox triage, like, figuring out which one's important versus which one can I just skip over and like get to later.
Um, we're, I've started to build my own kind of triage system.
I'm still checking with my CEO if he's going to allow me to do it, but.
with CEO.
Thank you.

If I take this role, won't matter anyway, but being able to kind of go through the inbox of like 40, 50 different emails that you get in a day and just being like, these are the three I actually need to respond to.
That would probably be where I would start.
Well, you'd be happy to know that we hate email very much.
And so we're all in Slack with all our customers.
Love it.
And so it helps.
mean, it gives additional challenges, which is like there's 50 million channels, but, you know.
I'll take Slack overload over email overload all day.
Yeah.
I'm with you on that one.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That's cool.
What, yeah, like what are you, maybe just the last one, most, you know, excited about potentially in this role in the company?
Yeah.
I mean, I think I told Tucker the first time I talked to him, like, if you ever offered a seat on a spaceship, you just ask which one.
And so.
Okay.
That's kind of my theory of joining you guys while you're kind of in hyper growth mode.

I think being very close to, you know, what we have tried to do at my current company of turning services into software makes it a really easy transition, at least from a mental model, to kind of see where you guys are at and where you're going.

And so I think just super passionate about, you know, my history and services, being able to kind of find ways to productize those to make it easier for, you the next account strategist that comes in.
And then I think really as the account strategist role, working directly with clients on kind of these day-to-day, you know, week-to-week, month-to-month, you know, what are we focusing on next?

How do we kind of push the envelope, not just from a sales and, you know, how do we get more revenue out of you guys, but how do we drive more revenue for you and shape your kind of direction for the future is, you know, super exciting to me.
I'm not from a...
Content background, but had no, you know, well enough to kind of be dangerous.
So I think also being able to come in and learn from experts and work side by side with them of kind of how are we doing the work?
How are we building these workflows and powering kind of growth for all these companies for the future is super exciting for me.
Yeah, that's awesome.
Well, I appreciate it.
Happy to also answer some questions for you, if there's any others that I didn't answer.
Yeah, think you answered two of the bigger ones, but I think the other ones, like from your perspective, what makes someone truly successful on this team beyond just like hitting metrics?
Yeah, yeah.
So Ada, love her to death.
She was hired as a managing editor in December and every one of her accounts that she was managing on the day-to-day.
So she wasn't this role specifically, right?
But she...
And I talked to her more recently.
like, what do you think made you successful?
And she's like, she'll come in and she didn't see herself as like, my job is to pump out content and call it a day.
Like, she's like, my job, first and foremost, it becomes best friend with the customer.
And as part of that, I am kind of like the forcing function, you know, I'm kind of babysitting, I'm pulling information, I'm the forcing function of everything to happen, you know.
And I think she just had this very different mentality of how she saw her role, but everybody else in her role didn't see it like that.
It's like, my job is to do this one task and this thing and just do it well.
And, you know, hopefully the client's happy with that, you know.
And so she kind of saw it very differently in the way she approached building trust as well was very, very unique.
And so that's really, really critical.
And then the last thing she said that I think was like really stuck with me too, is like, I never drop the ball.
So if I say I'm going to do something, I always do that thing.
And if I'm running behind, I'm going to tell them that I'm running behind on the thing and why I'm running behind.
And so it was very much like that, you know, it's like, how do you earn trust?
How do you build a deeper relationship with that customer?
How do you build empathy and knowledge for their business?
And yes, you got to do the job as well.

But so we promoted her to be part of the strategy sprints and she's scaling and she's overseeing like all of them and clients love her, like from CEOs to CMOs to anyone she works on and her bar for qualities really, really high in attention to detail, you know.
So I think those are some of the things are really, really good.
The George, who I just promoted as my chief of staff now, who's like kind of playing the role as well right now.

It's kind of the thing that's unique on top of what I mentioned with Ada is like he's very good at what I call input calibrator, which is asking the right questions and getting the right inputs from customers and stakeholders.
And then understanding, connecting the dots and be super reliable and organized as well.
so like asking good questions, I think is also pretty, pretty critical.
And yeah, like those are some of the things.
And I think at the end of the day is also just the resourcefulness.
You have the curiosity, but it's also the, you know, taking it to the finish line end to end.
And if you see something kind of getting stuck, not being like, yeah, stuck, somebody should probably look at that.
You know, that's a huge part.
Yeah.
I mean, working at a startup right now myself, like there's nobody else that's going to pick up the slack.
just drag it across the finish line.
Yeah.
Yeah, exactly.
Yeah.

Well, happy to answer one more if you, if you want, or if there's any areas, do you feel like I didn't cover that would help you give you a little bit insight into the company or culture or anything else?
No, I think I've had most questions answered along the way.
Okay.
And...
This has been an awesome, great conversation.
Maybe the last one is just how do you see the culture growing over the next year or as you guys continue to scale?
Yeah, I don't think we want to change the culture right now, although always evolving in opportunities to improve.
But I kind of set out to three principles for the company as far as like the number one is do the best work of our lives.
The second one is inspire everyone around you and, you know, which both internally and externally.
And it's been awesome to see like there's some customers that like quit their jobs and hit me up.
I was like, hey, I quit my job because I was so inspired to go do something and build it.
And, you know, like, and you helped me see that it was possible or, or like inspire internally.
man, like these people are so smart.
Like, this is awesome, you know?
And, and then the last one is like accomplish more than we thought was.
And if I think about, if you ask me three months ago, if we were to be where we are right now, I'd be like, oh, no way.
And if you ask me in January, where we would be, and I was like, no way.
And last year, it's like, hell no, like, no way.

And so it's like, there's like, if every increment of time period, you look back in that time period, and at the beginning of that time period, like, there's no way, like, I would imagine that we would have made this much progress or been where we are, then that's also a huge, huge sign, right, of like, of we're accomplishing more than we thought was possible based on where we were.

And so if those three things are right, and you're kind, and, you know, with ownership, you communicate with clarity, and some of the principles we have, then, then I think we're going to be in a really, really, really good place, you know, as a company, as in, and as individuals as well.
Awesome.
Love it.
Awesome, man.
Well, super excited.
I think we're very, very close here.
So we're going to move.
As fast as possible, get back to you ASAP.
All right.
Wonderful.
Sounds great.
Pleasure to meet you, Marcel.
Thanks, Erik.
Talk soon, Take care.
All right.
Bye.
